pages.title: Quasi-coherent sheaves on preschemes
pages.html:
II Application to quasi-coherent sheaves on preschemes

Proposition 1

Let \(X\) be a prescheme, \(Z\) a locally closed subset of the form \(Z=U-V\), where \(U\) and \(V\) are two open subsets of \(X\) such that \(V\subset U\) and such that the canonical immersions \(U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) are quasi-compact. Then for every quasi-coherent Module \(F\) on \(X\), the sheaves \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^i(F)\) are quasi-coherent.

By (I, 24), there exists an exact sequence of relative cohomology

\[ \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_U^{i-1}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_V^{i}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^{i}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_U^{i}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_V^{i+1}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}. \]

By (EGA III 1.4.17), in order for the \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^i(F)\) to be quasi-coherent it therefore suffices that the \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_U^i(F)\) and the \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_V^i(F)\) be so. We may therefore assume \(Z\) open and the canonical immersion \(j:Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) quasi-compact.

Since \(Z\) is open we have (I, 22) a canonical isomorphism:

\[ {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^{i}(F) \simeq \operatorname{R}^ij_{*}(F_{|Z}) \]

but \(j\) is separated (EGA I 5.5.1) and quasi-compact, hence (EGA III 1.4.10) the \(\operatorname{R}^ij_{*}(F|Z)={\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^{i}(F)\) are quasi-coherent, which completes the proof.

Corollary 2

Let \(Z\) be a closed subset of \(X\) such that the canonical immersion \(X-Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is quasi-compact; then the Modules \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^i(F)\) are quasi-coherent.

Corollary 3

If \(X\) is locally noetherian, then for every locally closed subset \(Z\) of \(X\), and every quasi-coherent Module \(F\) on \(X\), the \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^i(F)\) are quasi-coherent.

This follows immediately from Corollary 2 and from (EGA I 6.6.4).

Corollary 4

Suppose that \(X\) is the spectrum of a ring \(A\) and let \(U\) be a quasi-compact open set of \(X\), \(Y=X-U\), \(F\) a quasi-coherent Module on \(X\); then there exists an isomorphism of cohomological functors in \(F\):

\[ \tag{4.1} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^i(F) = \widetilde{(\operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F))}. \]

One has moreover an exact sequence functorial in \(F\):

\[ \tag{4.2} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(U, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^1(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

and isomorphisms functorial in \(F\):

\[ \tag{4.3} \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F) \simeq \operatorname{H}^{i-1}(U, F), \quad i\geqslant 2. \]

By (1), the \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^i(F)\) are quasi-coherent; since \(X\) is affine we therefore have \(\operatorname{H}^p(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^i(F))=0\) if \(p>0\). The spectral sequence (I, 23) degenerates, hence

\[ \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F) = \Gamma({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^i(F)). \]

The equality (4.1) then follows from (EGA I 1.1.3.7), (4.2) and (4.3) from the exact cohomology sequence (I(27)) and from the fact that \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F)=0\) if \(i>0\), since \(X\) is affine.

With the hypotheses of (4), \(U\) is a finite union of affine open sets \(X_f\); we can therefore find an ideal \(I\) generated by a finite number of elements \(f_{\alpha}\) and defining \(Y\), say \(\boldsymbol{f}=(f_{\alpha})\). With the notations of (EGA III 1) we have:

Proposition 5

Suppose that \(X\) is the spectrum of a ring \(A\), let \(\boldsymbol{f}=(f_{\alpha})\) be a finite family of elements of \(A\), \(Y\) the closed subset of \(X\) that they define, \(M\) an \(A\)-module, \(F\) the sheaf associated with \(M\). One then has isomorphisms of \(\partial\)-functors in \(M\):

\[ \tag{5.1} \operatorname{H}^i((\boldsymbol{f}), M) \simeq \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F). \]

(We shall also write \(\operatorname{H}_J^i(M)=\operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F)\), if \(Y\) is the closed subset of \(X=\operatorname{Spec} A\) defined by an ideal \(J\) of \(A\)).

For \(i=0\) and \(i=1\), we use the exact sequences (4.2) and (EGA III 1.4.3.2); if \(i\geqslant2\), we use (4.3) and (EGA III 1.4.3.2). This gives us isomorphisms functorial in \(M\). One checks that, up to a sign depending only on \(i\), they are compatible with the boundary operator, whence the existence of the isomorphism of \(\partial\)-functors (5.1).

Let now \(X\) be a prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\) and \(f:Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the inclusion, \(I\) a quasi-coherent ideal defining \(Y\) in \(X\). Let \(F\) be a sheaf on \(X\).

We have seen that there exist isomorphisms of \(\partial\)-functors in \(F\)

\begin{align*} \tag{*} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;f_{*}f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_X), F) &\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F) \\ \tag{**} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(f_{*}f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_X), F) &\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(F). \end{align*}

Let \(n, m\) be integers such that \(m\geqslant n\geqslant 0\); we denote by \(i_{n, m}\) the canonical map: \(\mathscr{O}_{Y_m}=\mathscr{O}_X/I^{m+1} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_X/I^{n+1}=\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}\), and by \(j_n\) the map: \(f_{*}f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}\). The \((\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, i_{n, m})\) form a projective system and the \(j_n\) are compatible with the \(i_{n, m}\).

Applying the functor \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;\cdot, F)\), one deduces from this a morphism

\[ \varphi': \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;f_{*}f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_X), F); \]

one shows easily that it is a morphism of cohomological functors in \(F\). The morphism

\[ \varphi: \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F), \]

composed of \(\varphi'\) and of (*), is therefore itself also a morphism of cohomological functors in \(F\).

One defines in the same way

\[ \underline{\varphi}: \varinjlim_{n} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^i(F). \]

We have in view the following theorem:

Theorem 6

a) Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\) defined by a coherent Ideal \(I\), \(F\) a quasi-coherent Module. Then \(\boldsymbol{\varphi}\) is an isomorphism.

b) If \(X\) is noetherian \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.

Theorem (6) will follow from (6).a and from the

Lemma 7

If the topological space underlying \(X\) is noetherian and if \(\boldsymbol{\varphi}\) is an isomorphism, the same holds for \(\varphi\).

We shall first prove (7). We know that there exists a spectral sequence

\[ \tag{7.1} \operatorname{H}^p(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^q(F)) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^{m}(X, F). \]

We have on the other hand an inductive system of spectral sequences

\[ \tag{7.2$_n$} \operatorname{H}^p(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F)) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^{m}(X;\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F). \]

It follows from the definition of \(\varphi\) and of \(\underline{\varphi}\) that these morphisms are associated with a homomorphism \(\Phi\) of spectral sequences from the inductive limit of (7.2_n) into (7.1). If the space underlying \(X\) is noetherian, by (God. 4.12.1)1

\[ \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{H}^p(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F))\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{H}^p(X, \varinjlim_{n} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F)), \]

then \(\Phi_2\) can be written as a morphism:

\[ \operatorname{H}^p(X, \varinjlim_{n} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^p(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^q(F)) \]

which is none other than the one deduced from \(\underline{\varphi}\).

If \(\underline{\varphi}\) is an isomorphism, the same therefore holds for \(\Phi_2\), hence for \(\varphi\) by (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}}\) 11.1.5); (7) is therefore proved.

We shall now prove (6.a); this is a local question on \(X\). By Corollary 4 and (EGA I 1.3.9 and 1.3.12) we may assume that \(X\) is the spectrum of a ring \(A\). It therefore suffices to prove that, under the hypotheses of Theorem 6.a), the canonical homomorphism:

\[ \tag{7.2} \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(A/I^n, M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, M) \]

is an isomorphism.

Let \(f_{\alpha}\) be a finite number of elements of \(A\) generating \(I\), \(\boldsymbol{f}=(f_{\alpha})\); then the sequence of ideals \((\boldsymbol{f}^n)\) is decreasing and cofinal with the sequence of the \(I^n\), so that (7.2) is equivalent to a morphism of \(\partial\)-functors in \(M\):

\[ \tag{7.3} \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(A/(\boldsymbol{f}^n), M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, M). \]

We have on the other hand canonical isomorphisms:

\[ \tag{7.4} \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Hom}_A(A/(\boldsymbol{f}^n), M) \simeq \varinjlim_{n} (m\in M\ |\ (\boldsymbol{f}^n)m=0) \simeq \operatorname{H}^0((\boldsymbol{f}), M). \]

Since \(\varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(A/(\boldsymbol{f}^n), M)\) is a universal \(\partial\)-functor in \(M\), there exists a single morphism of \(\partial\)-functors in \(M\):

\[ \tag{7.5} \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(A/(\boldsymbol{f}^n), M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i((\boldsymbol{f}), M), \]

which coincides in degree zero with (7.4).

Since the composite of (7.2) and of (5.1) is a morphism of \(\partial\)-functors in \(M\) which coincides with (7.5) in degree \(0\), it coincides with (7.5) in every degree. Theorem (6.a) is therefore an immediate consequence of the

Lemma 8

Let \(A\) be a noetherian ring, \(I\) an ideal generated by a finite system \(\boldsymbol{f}=(f_{\alpha})\) of elements, \(M\) an \(A\)-module. Then the homomorphisms (7.5) are isomorphisms.

Lemma 9

Let \(A\) be a ring, \(\boldsymbol{f}=(f_{\alpha})\) a finite system of elements of \(A\), \(I\) the ideal generated by \(\boldsymbol{f}\), \(i\) an integer \(>0\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. The homomorphism (7.5) is an isomorphism for every \(M\).

  2. \(\operatorname{H}^i((\boldsymbol{f}), M)=0\) for \(M\) injective.

  3. The projective system \((\operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{f}^n, A))=\operatorname{H}_{i, n}\) is essentially zero, that is to say: for every \(n\), there exists \(n'>n\) such that \(\operatorname{H}_{i, n'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{i, n}\) is zero.

a) implies b) trivially.

b) implies a); indeed b) implies that \(M\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\operatorname{H}^i((\boldsymbol{f}), M)\) is a universal cohomological functor, (7.5) is then a morphism of universal cohomological functors. It is an isomorphism in degree zero, hence in every degree

c) implies b); indeed if \(M\) is injective, one has for every \(n\)

\[ \operatorname{H}^i(\boldsymbol{f}^n, M)=\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{f}^n, A), M) = \operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{H}_{i, n}, M), \]

c) therefore implies that for every \(i\) the inductive system \((\operatorname{H}^i(\boldsymbol{f}^n, M))_{n\in\mathbf{Z}}\) is essentially zero, whence b).

b) implies c). Let indeed \(n>0\), and \(j\) a monomorphism of \(\operatorname{H}_{i, n}\) into an injective module \(M\). Let \(n'\geqslant n\) and let \(j_{n'}\in\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{H}_{i, n'}, M)\) be the composite of \(j\) and of the transition homomorphism \(t_{n', n}:\operatorname{H}_{i, n'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{i, n}\). The \(j_{n'}\) define an element of \(\operatorname{H}^i((\boldsymbol{f}), M)\) which is zero by hypothesis. There exists therefore \(n_0\) such that \(j_{n'}=0\) if \(n'>n_0\). But since \(j\) is a monomorphism, \(j_{n'}=0\) implies \(t_{n'}=0\), whence the proposition.

Corollary 10

Suppose that the space underlying \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) is noetherian. In order for the preceding conditions to be satisfied for every finite family of elements of \(A\) and every \(i>0\) (or again: for \(i=1\)), it is necessary and sufficient that for every injective \(A\)-module \(M\), the sheaf \(F\) associated with \(M\) be flasque.

It is necessary: let indeed \(\boldsymbol{f}=(f_{\alpha})\) be a finite system of elements of \(A\), \(Y\) the closed set defined by \(\boldsymbol{f}\) and \(U=X-Y\); one then has the exact sequence

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(U, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1((\boldsymbol{f}), M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

and thanks to 9.b, \(\operatorname{H}^0(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^0(U, F)\) is surjective.

It is sufficient by virtue of (5.1) and of the fact that for every closed subset \(Y\) of \(X\) and every flasque sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), \(\operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F)=0\) for \(i>0\).

Lemma 11

Under the hypotheses of Lemma 9, for every noetherian \(A\)-module \(N\) and every \(i>0\), the projective system \((\operatorname{H}_{i, n}(N))_{n\in\mathbf{Z}}\), where \(\operatorname{H}_{i, n}(N)=\operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{f}^n, N)\), is essentially zero.

Proof by induction on the number \(m\) of elements of \(\boldsymbol{f}\).

If \(m=1\), \(\boldsymbol{f}\) reduces to a single element, say \(f\); \(\operatorname{H}_{i, n}(N)\) is zero if \(i>1\) and \(\operatorname{H}_{1, n}(N)\) is canonically isomorphic to the annihilator \(N(n)\) of \(f^n\) in \(N\), the transition homomorphism \(N(n')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N(n)\), \(n'\geqslant n\), being multiplication by \(f^{n'-n}\). The \(N(n)\) form an increasing sequence of submodules of \(N\), and since \(N\) is noetherian there exists \(n_0\) such that \(N(n)=N(n_0)\) if \(n\geqslant n_0\). Hence all the \(N(n)\) are annihilated by \(f^{n_0}\) and the transition homomorphisms \(N(n')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N(n)\) are all zero if \(n'\geqslant n+n_0\). The lemma is therefore proved for \(m=1\).

We now suppose that \(m>1\) and that the lemma is proved for the integers \(m'<m\); let then \(\boldsymbol{g}=(f_1, \ldots, f_{m-1})\) and \(\boldsymbol{h}=f_m\).

For every \(n>0\), one has (EGA III 1.1.4.1) an exact sequence:

\[ 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_0(\boldsymbol{h}^n, \operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{g}^n, N)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{f}^n, N) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_1(\boldsymbol{h}^n, \operatorname{H}_{i-1}(\boldsymbol{g}^n, N)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

and, for \(n\) varying, a projective system of exact sequences. It follows from the induction hypotheses that for \(i>0\) the \(\operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{g}^n, N)\) form an essentially zero projective system, hence so do the \(\operatorname{H}_0(\boldsymbol{h}^n, \operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{g}^n, N))\) which one identifies with quotients of \(\operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{g}^n, N)\). For the terms on the right we shall factor the transition morphisms from \(n'\) to \(n\) through:

\[ \operatorname{H}_1(\boldsymbol{h}^{n'}, \operatorname{H}_{i-1}(\boldsymbol{g}^{n'}, N)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_1(\boldsymbol{h}^{n'}, \operatorname{H}_{i-1}(\boldsymbol{g}^{n}, N)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_1(\boldsymbol{h}^{n}, \operatorname{H}_{i-1}(\boldsymbol{g}^{n}, N)). \]

Since \(\operatorname{H}_{i-1}(\boldsymbol{g}^{n}, N)\) is a noetherian module it follows from the case \(m=1\) that there exists, for given \(n\), some \(n'>n\) such that the second arrow is zero. We thus see that in this projective system of exact sequences, the extreme projective systems are essentially zero, hence so is the middle projective system.

We have thus proved Lemma (11), hence Lemma (8) and consequently Theorem (6).

Remark

One can also obtain Theorem (6) by proving the condition of Corollary (10) by means of the structure theorems for injective modules over a noetherian ring (Matlis, Gabriel).

blocks.label: II
blocks.html: II Application to quasi-coherent sheaves on preschemes

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1

Let \(X\) be a prescheme, \(Z\) a locally closed subset of the form \(Z=U-V\), where \(U\) and \(V\) are two open subsets of \(X\) such that \(V\subset U\) and such that the canonical immersions \(U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), \(V\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) are quasi-compact. Then for every quasi-coherent Module \(F\) on \(X\), the sheaves \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^i(F)\) are quasi-coherent.
By (I, 24), there exists an exact sequence of relative cohomology

\[ \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}} olimits}_U^{i-1}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}} olimits}_V^{i}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}} olimits}_Z^{i}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}} olimits}_U^{i}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}} olimits}_V^{i+1}(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow}. \]

By (EGA III 1.4.17), in order for the \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}} olimits}_Z^i(F)\) to be quasi-coherent it therefore suffices that the \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}} olimits}_U^i(F)\) and the \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}} olimits}_V^i(F)\) be so. We may therefore assume \(Z\) open and the canonical immersion \(j:Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow}{ ightarrow} X\) quasi-compact.
Since \(Z\) is open we have (I, 22) a canonical isomorphism:
\[ {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^{i}(F) \simeq \operatorname{R}^ij_{*}(F_{|Z}) \]

but \(j\) is separated (EGA I 5.5.1) and quasi-compact, hence (EGA III 1.4.10) the \(\operatorname{R}^ij_{*}(F|Z)={\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^{i}(F)\) are quasi-coherent, which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2

Let \(Z\) be a closed subset of \(X\) such that the canonical immersion \(X-Z\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) is quasi-compact; then the Modules \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^i(F)\) are quasi-coherent.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3

If \(X\) is locally noetherian, then for every locally closed subset \(Z\) of \(X\), and every quasi-coherent Module \(F\) on \(X\), the \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Z^i(F)\) are quasi-coherent.
This follows immediately from Corollary 2 and from (EGA I 6.6.4).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4

Suppose that \(X\) is the spectrum of a ring \(A\) and let \(U\) be a quasi-compact open set of \(X\), \(Y=X-U\), \(F\) a quasi-coherent Module on \(X\); then there exists an isomorphism of cohomological functors in \(F\):

\[ \tag{4.1} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^i(F) = \widetilde{(\operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F))}. \]

One has moreover an exact sequence functorial in \(F\):

\[ \tag{4.2} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(U, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^1(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0 \]

and isomorphisms functorial in \(F\):

\[ \tag{4.3} \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F) \simeq \operatorname{H}^{i-1}(U, F), \quad i\geqslant 2. \]

By (1), the \({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^i(F)\) are quasi-coherent; since \(X\) is affine we therefore have \(\operatorname{H}^p(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^i(F))=0\) if \(p>0\). The spectral sequence (I, 23) degenerates, hence
\[ \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F) = \Gamma({\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^i(F)). \]

The equality (4.1) then follows from (EGA I 1.1.3.7), (4.2) and (4.3) from the exact cohomology sequence (I(27)) and from the fact that \(\operatorname{H}^i(X, F)=0\) if \(i>0\), since \(X\) is affine.

With the hypotheses of (4), \(U\) is a finite union of affine open sets \(X_f\); we can therefore find an ideal \(I\) generated by a finite number of elements \(f_{\alpha}\) and defining \(Y\), say \(\boldsymbol{f}=(f_{\alpha})\). With the notations of (EGA III 1) we have:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5

Suppose that \(X\) is the spectrum of a ring \(A\), let \(\boldsymbol{f}=(f_{\alpha})\) be a finite family of elements of \(A\), \(Y\) the closed subset of \(X\) that they define, \(M\) an \(A\)-module, \(F\) the sheaf associated with \(M\). One then has isomorphisms of \(\partial\)-functors in \(M\):

\[ \tag{5.1} \operatorname{H}^i((\boldsymbol{f}), M) \simeq \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F). \]
(We shall also write \(\operatorname{H}_J^i(M)=\operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F)\), if \(Y\) is the closed subset of \(X=\operatorname{Spec} A\) defined by an ideal \(J\) of \(A\)).

For \(i=0\) and \(i=1\), we use the exact sequences (4.2) and (EGA III 1.4.3.2); if \(i\geqslant2\), we use (4.3) and (EGA III 1.4.3.2). This gives us isomorphisms functorial in \(M\). One checks that, up to a sign depending only on \(i\), they are compatible with the boundary operator, whence the existence of the isomorphism of \(\partial\)-functors (5.1).

Let now \(X\) be a prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\) and \(f:Y\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) the inclusion, \(I\) a quasi-coherent ideal defining \(Y\) in \(X\). Let \(F\) be a sheaf on \(X\).
We have seen that there exist isomorphisms of \(\partial\)-functors in \(F\)

\begin{align*} \tag{*} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;f_{*}f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_X), F) &\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F) \\ \tag{**} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(f_{*}f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_X), F) &\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y^i(F). \end{align*}

Let \(n, m\) be integers such that \(m\geqslant n\geqslant 0\); we denote by \(i_{n, m}\) the canonical map: \(\mathscr{O}_{Y_m}=\mathscr{O}_X/I^{m+1} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_X/I^{n+1}=\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}\), and by \(j_n\) the map: \(f_{*}f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_X)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}\). The \((\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, i_{n, m})\) form a projective system and the \(j_n\) are compatible with the \(i_{n, m}\).
Applying the functor \(\operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;\cdot, F)\), one deduces from this a morphism

\[ \varphi': \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;f_{*}f^{-1}(\mathscr{O}_X), F); \]
one shows easily that it is a morphism of cohomological functors in \(F\). The morphism
\[ \varphi: \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(X;\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F), \]
composed of \(\varphi'\) and of (*), is therefore itself also a morphism of cohomological functors in \(F\).
One defines in the same way

\[ \underline{\varphi}: \varinjlim_{n} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}^i(\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^i(F). \]
We have in view the following theorem:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 6

a) Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed subset of \(X\) defined by a coherent Ideal \(I\), \(F\) a quasi-coherent Module. Then \(\boldsymbol{\varphi}\) is an isomorphism.

b) If \(X\) is noetherian \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
Theorem (6) will follow from (6).a and from the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 7

If the topological space underlying \(X\) is noetherian and if \(\boldsymbol{\varphi}\) is an isomorphism, the same holds for \(\varphi\).
We shall first prove (7). We know that there exists a spectral sequence
\[ \tag{7.1} \operatorname{H}^p(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^q(F)) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^{m}(X, F). \]
We have on the other hand an inductive system of spectral sequences

blocks.label: 7.2_n
blocks.html: \[ \tag{7.2$_n$} \operatorname{H}^p(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F)) \mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^{m}(X;\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F). \]

It follows from the definition of \(\varphi\) and of \(\underline{\varphi}\) that these morphisms are associated with a homomorphism \(\Phi\) of spectral sequences from the inductive limit of (7.2_n) into (7.1). If the space underlying \(X\) is noetherian, by (God. 4.12.1)1

\[ \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{H}^p(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F))\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \operatorname{H}^p(X, \varinjlim_{n} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F)), \]
then \(\Phi_2\) can be written as a morphism:

\[ \operatorname{H}^p(X, \varinjlim_{n} {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits}_{\mathscr{O}_X}^q(\mathscr{O}_{Y_n}, F)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^p(X, {\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits}_Y^q(F)) \]
which is none other than the one deduced from \(\underline{\varphi}\).
If \(\underline{\varphi}\) is an isomorphism, the same therefore holds for \(\Phi_2\), hence for \(\varphi\) by (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}}\) 11.1.5); (7) is therefore proved.

We shall now prove (6.a); this is a local question on \(X\). By Corollary 4 and (EGA I 1.3.9 and 1.3.12) we may assume that \(X\) is the spectrum of a ring \(A\). It therefore suffices to prove that, under the hypotheses of Theorem 6.a), the canonical homomorphism:
\[ \tag{7.2} \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(A/I^n, M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, M) \]
is an isomorphism.

Let \(f_{\alpha}\) be a finite number of elements of \(A\) generating \(I\), \(\boldsymbol{f}=(f_{\alpha})\); then the sequence of ideals \((\boldsymbol{f}^n)\) is decreasing and cofinal with the sequence of the \(I^n\), so that (7.2) is equivalent to a morphism of \(\partial\)-functors in \(M\):
\[ \tag{7.3} \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(A/(\boldsymbol{f}^n), M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, M). \]
We have on the other hand canonical isomorphisms:
\[ \tag{7.4} \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Hom}_A(A/(\boldsymbol{f}^n), M) \simeq \varinjlim_{n} (m\in M\ |\ (\boldsymbol{f}^n)m=0) \simeq \operatorname{H}^0((\boldsymbol{f}), M). \]
Since \(\varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(A/(\boldsymbol{f}^n), M)\) is a universal \(\partial\)-functor in \(M\), there exists a single morphism of \(\partial\)-functors in \(M\):
\[ \tag{7.5} \varinjlim_{n} \operatorname{Ext}_A^i(A/(\boldsymbol{f}^n), M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^i((\boldsymbol{f}), M), \]
which coincides in degree zero with (7.4).

Since the composite of (7.2) and of (5.1) is a morphism of \(\partial\)-functors in \(M\) which coincides with (7.5) in degree \(0\), it coincides with (7.5) in every degree. Theorem (6.a) is therefore an immediate consequence of the

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 8

Let \(A\) be a noetherian ring, \(I\) an ideal generated by a finite system \(\boldsymbol{f}=(f_{\alpha})\) of elements, \(M\) an \(A\)-module. Then the homomorphisms (7.5) are isomorphisms.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 9

Let \(A\) be a ring, \(\boldsymbol{f}=(f_{\alpha})\) a finite system of elements of \(A\), \(I\) the ideal generated by \(\boldsymbol{f}\), \(i\) an integer \(>0\). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. The homomorphism (7.5) is an isomorphism for every \(M\).

  2. \(\operatorname{H}^i((\boldsymbol{f}), M)=0\) for \(M\) injective.

  3. The projective system \((\operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{f}^n, A))=\operatorname{H}_{i, n}\) is essentially zero, that is to say: for every \(n\), there exists \(n'>n\) such that \(\operatorname{H}_{i, n'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{i, n}\) is zero.
a) implies b) trivially.

b) implies a); indeed b) implies that \(M\mathchoice{\longmapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}{\mapsto}\operatorname{H}^i((\boldsymbol{f}), M)\) is a universal cohomological functor, (7.5) is then a morphism of universal cohomological functors. It is an isomorphism in degree zero, hence in every degree
c) implies b); indeed if \(M\) is injective, one has for every \(n\)
\[ \operatorname{H}^i(\boldsymbol{f}^n, M)=\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{f}^n, A), M) = \operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{H}_{i, n}, M), \]
c) therefore implies that for every \(i\) the inductive system \((\operatorname{H}^i(\boldsymbol{f}^n, M))_{n\in\mathbf{Z}}\) is essentially zero, whence b).

b) implies c). Let indeed \(n>0\), and \(j\) a monomorphism of \(\operatorname{H}_{i, n}\) into an injective module \(M\). Let \(n'\geqslant n\) and let \(j_{n'}\in\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{H}_{i, n'}, M)\) be the composite of \(j\) and of the transition homomorphism \(t_{n', n}:\operatorname{H}_{i, n'}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}_{i, n}\). The \(j_{n'}\) define an element of \(\operatorname{H}^i((\boldsymbol{f}), M)\) which is zero by hypothesis. There exists therefore \(n_0\) such that \(j_{n'}=0\) if \(n'>n_0\). But since \(j\) is a monomorphism, \(j_{n'}=0\) implies \(t_{n'}=0\), whence the proposition.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 10

Suppose that the space underlying \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) is noetherian. In order for the preceding conditions to be satisfied for every finite family of elements of \(A\) and every \(i>0\) (or again: for \(i=1\)), it is necessary and sufficient that for every injective \(A\)-module \(M\), the sheaf \(F\) associated with \(M\) be flasque.
It is necessary: let indeed \(\boldsymbol{f}=(f_{\alpha})\) be a finite system of elements of \(A\), \(Y\) the closed set defined by \(\boldsymbol{f}\) and \(U=X-Y\); one then has the exact sequence

\[ \operatorname{H}^0(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^0(U, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}^1((\boldsymbol{f}), M) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]
and thanks to 9.b, \(\operatorname{H}^0(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\operatorname{H}^0(U, F)\) is surjective.
It is sufficient by virtue of (5.1) and of the fact that for every closed subset \(Y\) of \(X\) and every flasque sheaf \(F\) on \(X\), \(\operatorname{H}_Y^i(X, F)=0\) for \(i>0\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 11

Under the hypotheses of Lemma 9, for every noetherian \(A\)-module \(N\) and every \(i>0\), the projective system \((\operatorname{H}_{i, n}(N))_{n\in\mathbf{Z}}\), where \(\operatorname{H}_{i, n}(N)=\operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{f}^n, N)\), is essentially zero.
Proof by induction on the number \(m\) of elements of \(\boldsymbol{f}\).

If \(m=1\), \(\boldsymbol{f}\) reduces to a single element, say \(f\); \(\operatorname{H}_{i, n}(N)\) is zero if \(i>1\) and \(\operatorname{H}_{1, n}(N)\) is canonically isomorphic to the annihilator \(N(n)\) of \(f^n\) in \(N\), the transition homomorphism \(N(n')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N(n)\), \(n'\geqslant n\), being multiplication by \(f^{n'-n}\). The \(N(n)\) form an increasing sequence of submodules of \(N\), and since \(N\) is noetherian there exists \(n_0\) such that \(N(n)=N(n_0)\) if \(n\geqslant n_0\). Hence all the \(N(n)\) are annihilated by \(f^{n_0}\) and the transition homomorphisms \(N(n')\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N(n)\) are all zero if \(n'\geqslant n+n_0\). The lemma is therefore proved for \(m=1\).
We now suppose that \(m>1\) and that the lemma is proved for the integers \(m'<m\); let then \(\boldsymbol{g}=(f_1, \ldots, f_{m-1})\) and \(\boldsymbol{h}=f_m\).
For every \(n>0\), one has (EGA III 1.1.4.1) an exact sequence:

\[ 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_0(\boldsymbol{h}^n, \operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{g}^n, N)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{f}^n, N) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_1(\boldsymbol{h}^n, \operatorname{H}_{i-1}(\boldsymbol{g}^n, N)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

and, for \(n\) varying, a projective system of exact sequences. It follows from the induction hypotheses that for \(i>0\) the \(\operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{g}^n, N)\) form an essentially zero projective system, hence so do the \(\operatorname{H}_0(\boldsymbol{h}^n, \operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{g}^n, N))\) which one identifies with quotients of \(\operatorname{H}_i(\boldsymbol{g}^n, N)\). For the terms on the right we shall factor the transition morphisms from \(n'\) to \(n\) through:

\[ \operatorname{H}_1(\boldsymbol{h}^{n'}, \operatorname{H}_{i-1}(\boldsymbol{g}^{n'}, N)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_1(\boldsymbol{h}^{n'}, \operatorname{H}_{i-1}(\boldsymbol{g}^{n}, N)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{H}_1(\boldsymbol{h}^{n}, \operatorname{H}_{i-1}(\boldsymbol{g}^{n}, N)). \]

Since \(\operatorname{H}_{i-1}(\boldsymbol{g}^{n}, N)\) is a noetherian module it follows from the case \(m=1\) that there exists, for given \(n\), some \(n'>n\) such that the second arrow is zero. We thus see that in this projective system of exact sequences, the extreme projective systems are essentially zero, hence so is the middle projective system.
We have thus proved Lemma (11), hence Lemma (8) and consequently Theorem (6).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark

One can also obtain Theorem (6) by proving the condition of Corollary (10) by means of the structure theorems for injective modules over a noetherian ring (Matlis, Gabriel).

footnotes.html: Cf. first bibliographical reference, at the end of Exp. I.
